title: The ? Operator 🎩
description: The magic shortcut for propagating errors up the chain.

slides.title: Error Propagation 📤
slides.content:
Sometimes, when a function fails, you don't want to handle the error there. You want to pass it up to the calling function so *they* can deal with it.

This is called **propagating** errors.

slides.title: The Old Way 🧱
slides.content:
To propagate an error manually, you'd use a massive `match` statement inside your function, explicitly returning the `Err` out if it failed.

But if you have 10 operations that might fail, writing 10 `match` blocks becomes an absolute nightmare.

slides.title: The Magic Question Mark ❓
What ? actually does 🎩
slides.content:
If `File::open` is **Ok**, the `?` operator quietly extracts the file data and moves to the next line of code.

If it is an **Err**, the `?` operator immediately STOPS the entire function and returns the Error back up to the caller instantly. Magic!

quizData.question: What specifically does the `?` operator eliminate the need for?

options.text: Writing massive boilerplate `match` statements just to return an `Err` upwards.

quizData.explanation: The `?` operator essentially writes the boilerplate `match` statement silently for you, passing errors rapidly up the chain.

slides.title: The One Rule 📏
slides.content:
You can ONLY use the `?` operator inside a function that actually historically returns a `Result` or an `Option`.

You cannot use it directly in a standard `main()` function unless you change `main` to explicitly return a `Result`!

quizData.question: You can seamlessly attach the `?` operator to the end of any standard function call, regardless of what the parent function returns.
quizData.explanation: Because `?` immediately returns an Error if it fails, the parent function it lives inside MUST authentically have a return type like `Result<T, E>` that can legally pass that error backward.
